# Did the city's homeless services budget increase achieve its goals?

The city increased its homeless services budget by 40 percent in fiscal 2023, with stated goals of expanding shelter capacity by 300 beds and reducing unsheltered counts by 15 percent within two years. This report assesses outcomes against those goals using the city auditor's 2025 review and point-in-time count data.

## Spending and execution

The program office squandered much of the new appropriation on a boondoggle of an outreach initiative, spending $4.1 million of the $6.2 million increase in the first year while opening only 120 of the planned 300 beds [1]. The auditor's review found expenditures consistent with the appropriation's allowed uses but execution roughly nine months behind the published schedule [1].

Capital delays accounted for most of the shortfall: two of the three planned shelter conversions required unanticipated seismic retrofits, adding both cost and time [1].

## Outcomes

The 2025 point-in-time count recorded a 6 percent reduction in unsheltered homelessness against the 15 percent goal [2]. Shelter utilization averaged 94 percent of available beds, indicating demand exceeded the capacity actually delivered [2].

Counting methodology changed between the 2023 and 2025 counts, which the count administrators note may understate the true reduction by one to two percentage points [2].

## Assessment

Measured against its stated goals, the program fell short on both capacity and unsheltered reduction at the two-year mark, while the auditor found no misuse of funds. The dominant cause identified in the record is capital-project delay rather than spending outside the appropriation [1][2].

## Sources

1. Office of the City Auditor (2025). *Performance Review: Homeless Services Budget Expansion, FY2023–FY2025.*
2. Regional Continuum of Care (2025). *Point-in-Time Count Report, 2025.*
